---
title: "Project 1: predicting housing prices"
lang: en-US
author: 
- name: Daffy Duck
  email: daffy.duck@insee.fr
  affiliations: 
      - name: "[Insee](https://www.insee.fr/fr/accueil)"
- name: Mickey Mouse
  email: mickey.mouse@insee.fr
  affiliations: 
      - name: "[Insee](https://www.insee.fr/fr/accueil)"
format:
  html:
    number-sections: true
editor: visual
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{=html}
<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Technical level</th>
      <th>Tasks</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><span class="level-button level-beginner">Beginner</span></td>
      <td>Doing the exercises of steps 1 to 3, relying on clues and solutions if needed.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><span class="level-button level-intermediate">Intermediate</span></td>
      <td>Doing the exercises of steps 1 to 3, without relying on clues and solutions.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><span class="level-button level-expert">Expert</span></td>
      <td>Doing the exercises of steps 1 to 3, without relying on clues and solutions, and completing steps 4 (logging) and 5 (deployment).</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
```

# Introduction {.unnumbered}

The project focuses on predicting housing prices in the Paris area (Île-de-France region). We will explore how to perform inference using only a limited number of data sources, specifically transaction records and cadastral data. The reason for this choice is that data sources are widely available across many countries. At the end of this project, you will be able to :

-   prepare your training data;
-   train common machine learning (ML) models;
-   evaluate your models by choosing the most suited metric;
-   use some tools to ensure the reproductibility of the process (monitoring the model, deploy an API).

# Structure of the project

This project includes five steps (listed in the banner at the top of the page):

-   data generation : how to generate synthetic data from confidential data - this step can be skipped;
-   data preprocessing : how to prepare data input for the training of ML models;
-   model fitting and evaluation with two different models : Random Forest (RF) and Gradient Boosting (GB);
-   model logging with MLflow;
-   deployment.

Every step is independant from the other : one will be able to start from the next step if a step is too difficult.

# Initialization of the project

## Clone the project with `Git` {{< fa brands git-alt >}}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Caution

To launch in the project, open a VS Code service with the following button : <a href="https://datalab.sspcloud.fr/launcher/ide/vscode-python?name=AIML4OS-funathon-project1&version=2.5.2&s3=region-79669f20&git.cache=«5000»&git.repository=«https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2FAIML4OS%2Ffunathon-project1.git»&networking.user.enabled=true&autoLaunch=true" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><img src="https://custom-icon-badges.demolab.com/badge/SSP%20Cloud-Launch_with_VSCode-blue?logo=vsc&amp;logoColor=white" alt="Onyxia"/></a>.
:::

Create a VScode-Python service on SSP Cloud. In the service, open a terminal by clicking on {{< fa brands fa-bars >}}, then `Terminal > New Terminal`. Clone the project repository with:

```{bash}
git clone https://github.com/AIML4OS/funathon-project1.git
```

The project has the following structure :

-   The `.qmd` files and the `_quarto.yaml` file are necessary to build the website;
-   The file `pyproject.toml` describes the dependencies of the project (using uv);
-   Intermediate solutions to the exercices are available in the `intermediate_solutions` folder;
-   The final solution is available in the `final_solution` folder;
-   TO BE COMPLETED DEPENDING ON THE PROJECT (`Dockerfile`, `kubernetes`...)/

## Installation of dependencies

Install the project dependencies by running the following command in the terminal:

```{bash}
uv sync
```

If you need to install a new package, run the following command :

```{bash}
uv add <dependency_name>
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Watch out !

When running commands in the terminal, make sure you are working from the root of the Git repository.

You can verify your current location by checking the terminal prompt: it should end with *funathon2024_project1*. If it does not, navigate to the correct directory using the `cd` command.

*For example* :

-   **good path** ✅ :

``` bash
onyxia@vscode-python-882151-0:~/work/funathon-project1$ 
```

-   **bad path** ❌ :

``` bash
onyxia@vscode-python-882151-0:~/work$ 
```
:::

## Python interpreter configuration

After installing dependencies with `uv`, you need to configure the Python interpreter so that the installed packages are properly recognized. This will allow you to run Python scripts in parts, similar to working in a Jupyter notebook.

To do this, open the command palette `Show and Run commands` then select **`Python: select interpreter`** , or press `Ctrl+Shift+P`. Finally, enter the following path, which corresponds to the Python interpreter generated by `uv` :

``` bash
/home/onyxia/work/funathon-project1/.venv/bin/python3.13
```

# Presentation of the data

## List of variable and explanations

{{< include "temp/table_dict.qmd" >}}

## Remarks

### How to sign a

In France, a housing transaction is always formalized before a **notary**, who acts as a legal intermediary to ensure the transaction’s validity and compliance with civil law. The notary checks among other things that the property effectively belongs to the seller, calculates and collects the **transfer taxes** (such as *droits de mutation*) based on the transaction’s details and the property’s characteristics.

After collecting these taxes, the notary forwards the details of the transaction to the **fiscal administration**, including the sale price, the identities of the buyer and seller, the property’s cadastral reference and the notarized document. The fiscal administration then records this information in its databases, ensuring transparency and compliance with tax laws, while also updating the **land registry** to reflect the new ownership.

### Specificity of the data set

Since 2019, the fiscal administration publishes **all real estate transactions** twice a year in open data format on \<data.gouv.fr\>. You can access and download the original datasets (*Demande de Valeurs Foncières* or request for property values) directly here (in French only - sadly): [data.gouv.fr/datasets/demandes-de-valeurs-foncieres](https://www.data.gouv.fr/datasets/demandes-de-valeurs-foncieres). The published datasets contains less details on the property that have been sold. These details come from the land registry \[to be checked\]. That's why the data you will be working on is synthetic data. But part of the data directly comes from this published dataset.

**Types of transaction stored**:

What transaction is stored in the administrative dataset is a vast question. It's key to remember that the first goal of the dataset is to collect taxes. The dataset therefore is not fully complete and does not contain all the housing transactions in France that we would be interested in. The dataset remains of very high value and one of the most complete source of transaction as of today.\
For example, transactions involving **real estate investment company** (or *Sociétés Civiles Immobilières (SCIs)*) are excluded from housing transaction data, as they are not considered housing transactions from a legal perspective. However, in the way these companies are used transfering the ownership of a SCI is very similar to buying a property.

In addition, some transaction are registered in the data when they are of little interest to us.\
For example:

-   The dataset records partial transaction. When a property is jointly owned, the transaction does not specify the **share of ownership transferred**, as only the total sale price is typically recorded ;
-   The initial transfer of ownership from an individual to an SCI is recorded as a housing transaction, even if the SCI is usually owned by the same persons.

**Details over a flat**:

In the French housing market, we make a distinction between the **number of main rooms** and the **total number of rooms**.

The **number of main rooms** (*nombre de pièces principales*) refers specifically to habitable rooms, such as living rooms, bedrooms, dining rooms, and kitchens, and excludes rooms under 9 m², bathrooms, toilets, hallways, and storage spaces. It's the number of rooms that is the most commonly used in housing adds for example.

In contrast, the **total number of rooms** (*nombre total de pièces*) includes all rooms in a property, regardless of their size or function.

For example, a three-bedroom apartment with two bathrooms and a kitchen would have **three main rooms** but **six rooms** in total.

**Geographic coverage of the data** :

The dataset includes **overseas territories** such as Guadeloupe, Martinique, and Réunion, but excludes **Alsace-Moselle** (the departments of Bas-Rhin, Haut-Rhin, and Moselle). This exclusion is due to the **local civil law** (*droit local*), which applies in Alsace-Moselle because these territories were part of Germany from 1870 to 1918. It is not an issue as we will focus on a specific region but keep this in mind if you want to play with the data.

**Geographical codes** :

In France, the **department code** (a two-digit number, such as 75 for Paris) identifies the administrative department to which a city belongs.

The **official geographic code** — commonly referred to as the **Insee code** — is a unique five-digit identifier assigned by the National Institute of Statistics and Economic Studies ([Insee](https://www.insee.fr/en/metadonnees/source/serie/s2084)) to each city, commune, or administrative area. This code is the only reliable way to uniquely identify a city, as it remains constant even if the city’s name changes or if it merges with another municipality. The code is regularly updated to reflect administrative changes such as mergers or splits of municipalities. You can find the full COG data and documentation here: [Insee - Code Officiel Géographique (COG)](https://www.insee.fr/fr/information/2560452) and in open data format here: [data.gouv.fr - Code Officiel Géographique (COG)](https://www.data.gouv.fr/datasets/code-officiel-geographique-cog).

It means that if you search for a city with its Insee code on Google maps for example, Google maps won't find it as it uses the **zip code** (or postal code). The zip code is not a reliable identifier for a city as multiple cities share the same zip code, and a single city may have several zip codes depending on its districts or neighborhoods.

For example, the city of Paris is identified by the department code **75**, the Insee code **75056** (Paris itself), and is associated with multiple zip codes (e.g., **75001**, **75002**, etc.).
